coredata.dc:title: Functionalized graphene quantum dots obtained from graphene foams used for highly selective detection of Hg2+ in real samples
coredata.dc:creator: Llaver, Mauricio
Barrionuevo, Santiago D.
Prieto, Eduardo
Wuilloud, Rodolfo G.
Ibañez, Francisco J.
coredata.dc:description: Here we report the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), obtained from 3D graphene foam, functionalized with 8-hydroxyquinoline (8-HQ) for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ via front-face fluorescence. The great surface area and active groups within the GQDs permitted the functionalization with 8-HQ to increase their selectivity toward the analyte of interest. The fluorescence probe follows the Stern-Volmer model, yielding a direct relationship between the degree of quenching and the concentration of the analyte. Diverse parameters, including the pH and the use of masking agents, were optimized in order to improve the selectivity toward Hg2+ down to a limit of detection of 2.4 nmol L−1. It is hereby demonstrated that the functionalized GQDs work perfectly fine under adverse conditions such as acidic pH and in the presence of a large number of cationic and anionic interferences for the detection of Hg2+ in real samples. Parallel measurements using cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry also demonstrated an excellent correlation with the front-face fluorescence method applied here for real samples including tap, river, underground, and dam waters.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Representative Raman spectrum of graphene grown on Ni foam used as a precursor for obtaining GQDs (A). Deconvolution of 2D band resulted in four mode component characteristic of bilayer graphene. AFM image along with a representative cross-section for as-deposited GQDs (B). Different heights suggests that GQDs can be mono-, bi- and few-layered graphene as denoted by 1, 2, and 3; respectively.

ATR-FT-IR spectra for GQDs and GQDs functionalized with 8-hydroxyquinoline (A). XPS spectra of deconvoluted N 1s for 8HQ-functionalized GQDs (B). Excitation (λe m = 323 nm) and emission (λe x = 256 nm) spectra for as-synthesized and functionalized GQDs (red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Emission spectra (A) and relative emission intensity (I/I0) as a function of time (B) for a 0.0060 mg mL−1 solution of 8-hydroxyquinoline-functionalized GQDs before and after the addition of 100 nmol L−1 of Hg2+ (n = 3). Time = 0 represents a measurement before the addition of the analyte, and the maximum of this spectrum was considered as the reference intensity (I0). The inset in B shows that quenching occurs within the first 30 s.

Fluorescence effect on the relative emission intensity of 0.0060 mg mL−1 8-HQ-functionalized GQDs upon the addition of 25 μmol L−1 of various interfering species solutions measured at different pH values (n = 3).
Relative emission intensity of as-obtained (A) and 8-HQ-modified GQDs (B) before and after the addition of 100 nmol L−1 of Hg2+, and upon the addition of 25 μmol L−1 of various cations and anions.

Relative emission intensity of a 0.0060 mg mL−1 solution of 8-hydroxyquinoline-functionalized GQDs as a function of Hg2+ concentration, with the linear range shown as an inset (n = 3). The panel on the right shows the actual emission spectra (quenching) corresponding to the linear region from 0 to 200 nmol L−1.
Comparison of our results with recently published GQD-based fluorescent sensors employed for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Functionalized graphene quantum dots obtained from graphene foams used for highly selective detection of Hg2+ in real samples

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here we report the use of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), obtained from 3D graphene foam, functionalized with 8-hydroxyquinoline (8-HQ) for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ via front-face fluorescence. The great surface area and active groups within the GQDs permitted the functionalization with 8-HQ to increase their selectivity toward the analyte of interest. The fluorescence probe follows the Stern-Volmer model, yielding a direct relationship between the degree of quenching and the concentration of the analyte. Diverse parameters, including the pH and the use of masking agents, were optimized in order to improve the selectivity toward Hg2+ down to a limit of detection of 2.4 nmol L−1. It is hereby demonstrated that the functionalized GQDs work perfectly fine under adverse conditions such as acidic pH and in the presence of a large number of cationic and anionic interferences for the detection of Hg2+ in real samples. Parallel measurements using cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry also demonstrated an excellent correlation with the front-face fluorescence method applied here for real samples including tap, river, underground, and dam waters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is one of the most dangerous chemical elements for humans [1] and the environment [2], due to its relative solubility in water and high volatility in the atmosphere. Prolonged exposure to this element, even at low concentrations, has serious negative effects on human health [3]. Mercury can be found in the environment as elemental Hg, as methylmercury in biota, and as Hg2+ in water, soil, and sediments, making the latter species the most relevant to be detected [4]. In this regard, several analytical methods and techniques have been developed and combined for the quantification of these species, including dispersive solid-phase microextraction coupled to X-ray fluorescence spectrometry [5], liquid-liquid-based microextraction coupled to UV–Vis spectroscopy [6], solid-phase extraction coupled to cold vapor-atomic fluorescence spectrometry (CV-AFS) [7], electrocatalysis [8] and magnetic solid-phase extraction coupled to cold-vapor atomic absorption spectrometry [9]. Although these techniques are quite sensitive and reliable, they are costly, complex to operate, and usually time-consuming due to the required samples pretreatment.

Recently, fluorescent sensors have appeared as interesting alternatives to overcome these limitations, especially considering their association with sustainable applications, simple sample preparation, and flexibility to be chemically modified. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) fulfill most of the aforementioned attributes due to quantum confinement effects and the possibility of chemical functionalization [10]. Most of those characteristics have been extended to applications in biomedicine [11], imaging [12], catalysis [13], optoelectronics [14], and sensing [15], to mention just a few. GQDs can be obtained via bottom-up or top-down approaches. The former involves mainly solution-based chemical syntheses, which have the advantage of being able to build up the carbon nanostructures molecule-by-molecule [16], leading to highly reproducible nanomaterials, whose characteristics strongly depend on the pre- and post-preparation conditions [17]. Top-down methods, on the other hand, involve the chemical, electrochemical or thermal breakdown of larger graphitic structures using acidic exfoliation, ion-bombardment, and microwave or UV radiation, among others [18]; leading most of the time to a poor control over the product [19]. Electrochemical exfoliation from graphene becomes a very promising alternative in this context, since the obtained product may be comprised of crystalline and highly pure GQDs, meanwhile avoiding harsh chemical conditions [20]. Despite that, GQDs generated from graphene in a 2D [21] and 3D [22] configuration have been scarcely explored. Chen and co-workers performed the first electrochemical exfoliation of 3D graphene to obtain carbon nanostructures for the detection of Fe3+ ions, but their synthesis involved the use of organic solvents and ionic liquids [22]. Later, Messina et. al also obtained GQDs from 3D graphene in a similar manner; however, the synthesis was performed in polar solvents [23].

Due to the presence of functional groups, GQDs comprise a mixture of sp2 and sp3 hybridized carbon atoms which are responsible for their HOMO-LUMO bandgap. Unlike graphene, this bandgap leads to extraordinary photoluminescent properties [24], while the 2D surface can take part in covalent [25] or non-covalent [26] interactions with molecules or ions, which ultimately determine their emission properties [27]. This has led to extensive applications in fluorescence sensing, as alternatives to organic fluorophores [28], metal nanoclusters [29], and semiconducting quantum dots [30]. This alternative is especially important considering some known drawbacks associated with low emissions and poor quantum yield suffered by the aforementioned probes [31], as well as the negative environmental impact caused by the use of semiconducting quantum dots (e.g. CdTe, WO3-x) [32].

In this context, great efforts have been made to improve the sensitivity of GQDs for fluorescence sensing. Nevertheless, the detection of a single analyte still remains challenging, because the probe is usually involved in systematic interactions with the analyte and with organic/inorganic interferences present within the sample, making the detection quite complex [33]. For instance, to improve the selectivity for the determination of Hg2+, it has been shown that GQDs need to be chemically functionalized or doped, as explained in this Review [34]. For example, valine- [35], glycine- [36], folic acid- [37], l-cysteine- [38], and Tb(III)- [26] functionalized and N- [30], N- and S- [39], and N- and B- [40] doped GQDs have been used for the determination of Hg2+, resulting in probes which outperform pristine carbon nanostructures. However, there still remains room for improvements, especially concerning the control over the properties of the product and its purification process [19], which are key aspects toward any application.

In this work, we thoroughly characterize and explore an 8-hydroxyquinoline (8-HQ)-functionalized GQD-based fluorescent probe for a fast, sensitive, and selective detection of Hg2+ in real samples. GQDs were obtained via a relatively easy and clean top-down electrochemical approach followed by functionalization with 8-HQ to increase the selectivity toward the analyte. The functionalized nanomaterial was exhaustively characterized by several techniques and successfully applied for the fast quantification of Hg2+ in river, dam, underground, and tap waters using front-face fluorescence. To achieve high standards of selectivity and sensitivity, we carried out studies for selecting the optimal pH and masking agent. To the best of our knowledge, this report represents the first application of graphene foam-based, electrochemically-obtained GQDs for the detection of Hg2+ in real samples.

Nickel foam with thickness = 1.6 mm and porosity = 87% was purchased from MTI Corp (Richmond, CA, USA). H2 (99.999%) and CH4 (99.999%) were purchased locally from Linde, Argentina. 8-hydroxyquinoline (99%), absolute ethanol (99.5%) and sodium hydroxide (>98%), were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA) while acetone (99.8%) was from J.T. Baker (Xalostoc, Mexico). Hydrochloric acid (37% v/v) and sodium nitrate (NaNO3, ≥99.0%) were from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA, 99%) was from Biopack (Buenos Aires, Argentina). A 1003 mg L−1 Hg2+ standard solution from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany) was used for the preparation of working solutions. Standard solutions for interferences measurements were obtained from Merck and J.T. Baker (Phillipsburg, NJ, USA). Sodium borohydride (NaBH4), from Merck, was used as reducing agent for the CV-AFS measurements.

All fluorescence measurements were carried out with a RF-6000 spectrofluorophotometer (Shimadzu, Kyoto, Japan). Front-face fluorescence spectra were obtained by placing a quartz cuvette in a solid sample holder which included a light cutting filter for blocking scattered light. Emission and excitation spectra were smoothed using the Savitzky-Golay method with a 5-point window and a polynomial order of 2, using the OriginPro 2021 software (OriginLab Corporation). Excitation-emission matrices were obtained using the corresponding built-in method included in the LabSolutions RF software (Shimadzu). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) images were obtained using a Nanoscope V (Veeco) microscope operating in tapping mode. Raman spectroscopy was performed in a LABRAMHR 800 Horiba Jobin Yvon confocal microscope employing a 514 nm laser beam and a 100 x (0.9 NA) objective lens to collect Raman spectra. XPS data were collected using Al Kα radiation at 1486.69 eV (150 W, 10 mA), a charge neutralizer and a delay line detector consisting of three multi-channel plates. Survey spectra were recorded from −5 to 1350 eV at a pass energy of 150 eV (number of sweeps: 2) using an energy step size of 1 eV and a dwell time of 100 ms. High resolution spectra for C 1s, and N 1s were recorded in the appropriate regions, using an energy step size of 0.1 eV. Attenuated Total Reflectance-Fourier Transform-Infrared (ATR-FT-IR) spectra were obtained using a Perkin Elmer (Beaconsfield, UK) Spectrum Two instrument with a Universal ATR module from the same company. For CV-AFS measurements, an AF-640A Rayleigh atomic fluorescence spectrometer (Beijing Rayleigh Analytical Instrument Corp., Beijing, China) was used, with a hollow-cathode Hg lamp (Beijing General Research Institute for Ferrous Non-Metals, Beijing, China). A Horiba F-51 pH meter (Kyoto, Japan) was used for pH determinations. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (APEMA, Buenos Aires, Argentina).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical exfoliation of graphene to form GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs were obtained based on the electrochemical exfoliation of graphene already grown on Ni foam by a well-known chemical vapor deposition method [41,42]. The electrochemical cell consisted of a two electrode set-up based on 3D graphene and Cu foil acting as anode and cathode, respectively. Both electrodes were immersed into a 0.1 mol L−1 NaOH ethanoic (96% v/v in water) electrolyte, and further subjected to 30 V for 1 h. At the beginning the dispersion remained colorless, and then turned yellow toward the end of the synthesis [43]. The resulting dispersion was centrifuged at 1090×g for 30 min to eliminate any solid residues, and the solvent mixture was evaporated at 60 °C. The remaining solid was then dissolved in 15 mL of absolute ethanol under stirring, followed by centrifugation for 15 min, elimination of the solid residues and evaporation of the solvent at 60 °C. This was repeated four times and the remaining solid was stored at 4 °C.

GQDs functionalized with 8-HQ (8-HQ-GQDs) were obtained following the report by Dahaghin et al., who functionalized magnetized graphene oxide with the aforementioned molecule [44]. In this case, 120.0 mg of as-synthesized GQDs were dispersed in 120 mL of ultrapure water and mixed with 120 mL of a saturated aqueous solution of 8-HQ, obtained after 2 h of ultrasonication at room temperature with excess 8-HQ and filtration through a 0.45 μm pore size PTFE syringe filter (Millipore Corporation). The resulting mixture was stirred for 48 h and all the remaining solids were eliminated by centrifugation, followed by the evaporation of the supernatant solvent at 60 °C. The resulting solid product was then washed three times using 15 mL of acetone in order to dissolve any remaining 8-HQ, followed by centrifugation (1090×g, 5 min). Finally, the remaining solid was dried at 60 °C for 1 h to remove all traces of acetone, and stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Collection of real samples and treatment
ce:section.ce:para: All samples were collected directly in clean HDPE bottles, except for tap water, that was left to run for 20 min prior to sampling. 500 mL of dam and river waters were obtained from the Potrerillos dam (Mendoza, Argentina) and the La Carolina River (San Luis, Argentina), at 5 cm below the water surface. Both samples were centrifuged to eliminate residues and then filtered through 0.45 μm pore size PTFE membrane filters and stored at 4 °C. Underground water was obtained from a well in Lavalle City (Mendoza, Argentina), filtered and stored at 4 °C. In all cases, fluorescence analyses were run on 5 mL aliquots of the pre-treated samples, or on standard samples for optimization studies. 90 μL of 37% (v/v) HCl were added to the samples, along with 200 μL of 0.01 mol L−1 EDTA and 10 μL of a 3.00 mg mL−1 solution of 8-HQ-GQDs, and the mixtures were immediately measured by front-face fluorescence with the instrumental set-up described in Section 2.2. During the measurements, instrumental excitation and emissions slits were fixed at 5.0 and 20 nm, respectively, to maximize the sensitivity. The excitation wavelength was set at 260 nm and emission spectra were collected from 300 to 480 nm at room temperature. The scan rate was selected at 600 nm min−1 in all measurements.

Study of the Raman bands from graphene (before electrochemical exfoliation) provide better structural information than those from GQDs, due to the presence of the characteristic 2D band associated with the number of layers and the overall quality of the starting material. Fig. 1 A shows a representative Raman spectrum of 3D graphene grown on Ni foam, used as a precursor for obtaining GQDs. Some particular aspects such as the G to 2D band ratio and the 2D full-width at half-maximum (FWHD) of 0.8 and 54 cm−1, respectively, indicate that graphene grew into 2 or more layers [42]. In addition, a four-component deconvolution of the 2D band (2D1A, 2D1B, 2D2A, 2DBA) shown in Fig. 1B clearly indicates that graphene grew into at least 2 layers. Our group has performed statistics based on Raman experiments and demonstrated that even though graphene can occasionally grow into a monolayer, the outcome product is usually comprised of 2 or more layers. Fig. 1C shows an AFM image of as-deposited GQDs on atomically flat mica along with a representative cross-section indicating heights values ranging from ∼0.4 (monolayer) to 2.5 nm (few layers). These AFM heights are consistent with Raman experiments which indicated that as-deposited GQDs are comprised of at least bi-layered graphene.

Fig. 2 A shows ATR-FT-IR spectra of as-obtained GQDs and 8-HQ-modified GQDs, with several features that need to be highlighted. First, there is a marked decrease in the intensity of the GQD signals at 1429 and 881 cm−1 after functionalization, which correspond to C–O stretching and C–C vibrations from epoxy groups, respectively [45]. This could indicate that the bonding of 8-HQ occurs via an epoxide ring-opening reaction. Second, the appearance of new bands at approximately 1150, 1250 and 1575 cm−1, ascribed to C–N, C–O and CN bonds, respectively, indicate the presence of 8-HQ. It should be noticed that the signal at approximately 1250 cm−1 comes from the phenol ring present in the 8-HQ complex [44]. This is consistent with Fig. 2B, that shows XPS spectra of the deconvoluted N 1s band at 399.7 eV, characteristic of amino-containing groups in the 8-HQ-functionalized nanomaterial. The peaks observed at 399.7, 404.2, and 406.9 are assigned to pyridinic N, C–NC, and N–O; respectively. On the other hand, the XPS spectra of pristine 8-HQ (See Fig. S1 of the Electronic Supporting Information, ESI) exhibits a single peak at 399.8 assigned to pyridinic N [46–48]. In summary, the XPS spectrum confirms the presence of pyridinic N in our functionalized samples and the observed N–O pyridinic oxide peak strongly suggests that 8-HQ is bonded through C–O groups at the edges/defects of the carbon nanostructure.

Fig. 2C exhibits excitation and emission spectra of 0.0060 mg mL−1 aqueous solutions of GQDs before and after their functionalization with 8-HQ. The optimal emission and excitation wavelengths (fixed at 323 and 256 nm, respectively) were obtained from the corresponding excitation-emission matrices, which can be found in Fig. S2 of the ESI. Fig. 2C exhibits slight red-shifts for both the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths, as indicated. These shifts can be associated to the presence of electron donor groups located at the edges of the GQDs, such as the oxygenated moieties resulting from the functionalization with 8-HQ. The presence of these groups produces a decrease in the HOMO-LUMO energy gap due to the increase in electron density, which is ultimately reflected in the changes observed in the spectra [49]. It should be pointed out that the maximum emission intensity remains practically identical after the functionalization, which suggests that the presence of 8-HQ would not further modify the emission characteristics of the probe rather than the previously observed red-shift.

Fig. 3 A shows the emission spectra of 8-HQ-GQDs with and without 100 nmol L−1 of Hg2+, while Fig. S3 in the ESI shows pictures of these solutions under ambient light and under a low-power, UV-C commercial lamp. Fig. 3A exhibits a 65% drop of fluorescence intensity upon the incorporation of the analyte. The enhanced quenching of 8-HQ-GQDs with respect to unfunctionalized GQDs (See Fig. S4 in ESI) could be ascribed to the strong chelating interaction between 8-HQ and the analyte. This, in turn, can be responsible for accelerating the non-radiative recombination of the generated exciton via an electron transfer mechanism, thus leading to a more pronounced quenching, as it has been demonstrated for several metallic cations [35,50]. This type of interaction is known to reduce the HOMO-LUMO gap of GQD-based probes, which increases the probability of attaining non-radiative relaxation processes while red-shifting the emission as the concentration of quencher increases [51]. This can be verified in the spectra of 8-HQ-GQDs in the presence of different Hg2+ concentrations, shown later on in Fig. 6, where it can be seen that as the analyte concentration increases, the emission intensity is reduced and the emission band is shifted toward higher wavelengths. In addition, this rules out the possibility of an aggregation-induced quenching mechanism, for which the emission intensity decreases as the concentration of quencher increases, albeit without affecting the maximum emission wavelength, as has been corroborated for different analytes [22,52,53].

So far, it is qualitatively demonstrated that 8-HQ-GQDs could act as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for the selective detection of Hg2+ based on the quenching effect produced by the analyte over the probe. Regarding quantitative aspects, it was found that the quenching of the 8-HQ-GQDs emission in the presence of Hg2+ followed the classical Stern-Volmer model. This model describes the turn-off intensity as a bimolecular process in which the quencher concentration is considered constant (pseudo-first order conditions), leading to a mathematical expression that correlates the ratio of fluorescence intensities with the added quencher concentration:
(Equation 1)

S V

where; I
o is the emission intensity of the fluorescent probe in absence of quencher, I is the emission intensity of the probe in the presence of a given concentration of quencher, K
SV is the Stern-Volmer constant (associated with the product of the bimolecular quenching rate constant and the fluorescence lifetime of the probe in absence of the quencher) and [Q] is the concentration of quencher [54]. This model yields a simple and linear relationship between the ratio of intensities and the concentration of the quencher, which is ideal for the quantification of Hg2+.

Taking into account the above-described model, we first studied the effect of the concentration of 8-HQ-GQDs on the fluorescence response, which may affect the linear range of the probe. In fact, concentrations of 0.0600 and 0.0060 mg mL−1 of 8-HQ-GQDs led to a linear detection of Hg2+ in the order of μM and nM concentrations, respectively, as discussed later on in Section 3.4. This is a consequence of the “turn-off” effect of the analyte on the probe, which is masked when the emission intensity of the latter is too high. In this regard, it is important to point out that 8-HQ-GQDs concentrations below 0.0060 mg mL−1 resulted useless to achieve reproducible measurements.

The response time of the 8-HQ-GQDs toward Hg2+ was also evaluated and measured for solutions with final analyte concentrations of 100 nmol L−1. Fig. 3B exhibits a plot of I/Io vs time, indicating that the emission intensity of the probe readily decreased upon addition of Hg2+, and remained constant throughout at least 100 min. This demonstrates that the interaction responsible for the quenching occurs within the first 30 s after mixing the solutions. This represents a key benefit of the 8-HQ-GQDs since a rapid analysis is always highly desired. At a first glance, these results show an excellent stability for the detection of Hg2+, as demonstrated even after 100 min from the addition of the probe.

We conducted further stability studies and found that solutions stored for over 4 months at 4 °C revealed no apparent changes to the emission spectra. These results demonstrate that the probe is highly stable and ready to use on a daily basis. On the other hand, exposure of the probe to UV light (15 W, UV-C lamp) showed that 8-HQ-GQDs dispersions suffered from bleaching under these conditions, which resulted in 20 and 32% decrease in their emission intensity after 10 and 60 min, respectively. We believe this result would not limit the analytical application, since the probe is not actually exposed for such prolonged times during the measurements.

Finally, the effect of the presence of salts was evaluated, using NaNO3 as a model saline compound. For this, the emission of 0.0060 mg L−1 solutions of 8-HQ-GQDs prepared at pH = 1 was measured at different salt concentrations. The results showed that NaNO3 concentrations larger than 2 g L−1 affected the emission characteristics of the probe. This is in good agreement with the fact that real samples, of relatively low salinity, could be efficiently analyzed, as described in Section 3.6, whereas seawater samples, could not be properly analyzed. In light of these results, it is demonstrated that the probe is quite sensitive and stable upon the detection of Hg2+, although its selectivity still required further improvement, as addressed in the following section.

In order to improve the selectivity of 8-HQ-GQDs, we first studied the effect of five common species, i.e. Cu2+, Fe3+, Ca2+, Na+ and Pb2+, which usually interfere in the fluorescent determination of Hg2+. Fig. 4 shows a plot of relative fluorescence intensity vs. pH for 8-HQ-GQDs exposed to 25 μmol L−1 of the aforementioned species. The Figure shows that the quenching effect of these cationic species strongly depends on the pH of the medium, while also evidencing an improved selectivity at low pH (between 1.0 and 4.0), where only Hg2+ and Fe3+ were detected. This strongly correlates to the chelating properties of 8-HQ, which is highly selective toward both analytes Hg2+ and Fe3+ under similar pH conditions [55]. Interestingly, this also demonstrates that the chelating properties of 8-HQ remain unaffected even after its anchoring onto the surface of the GQDs, probably due to the availability of the N and O atoms present along the complexing agent structure. In conclusion, this demonstrates that the pH of the medium plays a key role upon improving the selectivity of the probe.

Further interference experiments were carried out in the presence of several other cationic and anionic species. Fig. 5 A and B show the effect on the fluorescence emission for as-synthesized and 8-HQ-modified GQDs before and after the addition of 100 nmol L−1 of Hg2+, and upon the addition of 25 μmol L−1 of potentially interfering cations and anions. All the experiments were carried out at pH = 1. It can be clearly seen in Fig. 5A that the majority of the added species have a significant effect on the emission intensity of the GQDs, either by quenching or, in some other minor cases, by increasing the fluorescence intensity. This demonstrates that the unmodified carbon nanostructure is sensitive toward multiple species, including Ag+, Mg2+ and Al3+, among others. The lack of selectivity is most likely due to the presence of O-containing groups along the surface of the GQDs (i.e.; ether and epoxide groups, as seen by the ATR-FT-IR characterization), which can act as non-specific chelating moieties for cations.

The functionalization with 8-HQ, on the other hand, provided the highly desired selectivity to the GQDs, as shown in Fig. 5B. It is thereby shown that, amongst the vast array of analyzed species, only Hg2+ and Fe3+ caused significant changes in the emission intensity of the functionalized material, therefore representing an enormous improvement in terms of selectivity. It is demonstrated then that 8-HQ molecules within the GQDs play a key role for the high selectivity achieved toward Hg2+ and Fe3+ at low pH values. It should be highlighted also that the evaluated anions did not cause any change in the response of the GQDs, either with or without functionalization with 8-HQ. This is reasonable, since the probe-analyte interaction is of a coordinating character, hence only involving cationic species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Further improvements in the selectivity of 8-HQ-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As evidenced by the previous results, at pH = 1, Fe3+ remained as the only species interfering with the detection of Hg2+. To surpass this issue, we introduced EDTA as a masking agent, due to its well-known favorable complexing equilibrium constant with Fe3+, which is four orders of magnitude higher than that with Hg2+ [56]. Figure S5 (ESI) shows the effect of EDTA concentrations ranging from 1.0 × 10−5 to 2.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 on the relative emission intensity of 8-HQ-GQDs, with and without 100 nmol L−1 Hg2+, in the presence of 25 μmol L−1 Fe3+. The Figure shows that concentrations over 5.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 and up to 2.0 × 10−3 mol L−1 of EDTA could effectively mask the effect of Fe3+, without interfering with either the emission intensity of the probe or the quenching effect produced by the analyte. Accordingly, 5.0 × 10−4 mol L−1 EDTA was systematically used in further experiments.

Fig. 6 shows the fluorescence response of the sensor (tested at the optimized conditions described above) in the presence of different concentrations of the analyte, along with the calibration curve obtained from the linear portion of the plot (shown as an inset). The panel next to Fig. 6 exhibits the actual emission spectra of the probe in the presence of increasing concentrations of Hg2+, taken within the linear range between 0 and 200 nmol L−1. The response of the probe follows the Stern-Volmer model (Equation (1)), hence presenting a linear relationship between I
o
/I and the concentration of Hg2+. As can be seen in Fig. 6, the relative fluorescence intensity increases sharply (as a consequence of the decrease in the emission of the probe upon the addition of Hg2+) for concentrations in a range between the LOD and up to 200 nmol L−1, and then starts to flatten out as the concentration of Hg2+ increases beyond this point. Considering solely the linear region of the plot, the regression fit yielded the equation I
o
/I = (0.999 ± 0.008) + (0.0103 ± 0.0002) C, where I
o
/I is the relationship between the maximum fluorescence intensity of pure 8-HQ-GQDs and the maximum fluorescence intensity of 8-HQ-GQDs in the presence of the different analyte concentrations, and C is the concentration of Hg2+ in nmol L−1. The R2 correlation coefficient of the regression corresponds to 0.9982, indicating a very acceptable linear fit of the data.

The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from the linear fit, as 3 times the quotient of the standard deviation of the intercept and the slope, which resulted in a value of 2.4 nmol L−1. In a similar fashion, the limit of quantification was calculated as 10x the same relationship, resulting in a value of 8.1 nmol L−1. It is important to highlight that both values stand below the 10 nmol L−1 of Hg2+ in waters permitted for human consumption, as stablished by the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA). This is a good indication of the applicability of the probe for the identification of legally-determined dangerous concentration levels of the analyte [35]. Furthermore, in order to evaluate the reproducibility of the probe, 6 independent measurements were performed at a probe concentration of 0.0060 mg mL−1 for the detection of 100 nmol L−1 of Hg2+. A relative standard deviation of 1.6% was obtained for these results, demonstrating an excellent precision.

Table 1 summarizes the most recently reported fluorescent GQD-based sensors for Hg2+, including ours. The table shows the type of fluorescence sensor, synthesis method, linear range, LOD, and the sample source. The LOD achieved by our probe outperforms most of the values listed in the Table. This is especially outstanding when considering that some reports did not even measure Hg ions in real samples [51,57]. It should be noticed that only half of the recently reported methods (including ours) involve the detection of Hg2+ in real environmental samples, which goes way beyond in terms of difficulty when compared to synthetic standard solutions and tap water analyses. Interestingly, there is one report that detects Hg2+ in human plasma samples; however, the probe lacks selectivity, since it simultaneously responded to 7 other cationic analytes [58]. This clearly demonstrates the difficulty in adapting this family of probes to more complex chemical environments, which represents one of the main challenges for the development of fluorescent GQD-based methods. Additionally, all of the referenced methods involve bottom-up wet chemistry syntheses, which usually require harsh conditions [58] and exhaustive purification steps such as, for instance, 48-h dialysis [59,60]. A drawback from our probe relays on the linear range, which does not reach concentrations as high as some of the works compared here [59,61].

Regarding other traditional methods for the quantification of Hg2+, the main advantage of our development is related to the simple sample treatment required prior to the fluorescence measurement. Spectrophotometric methods, for example, usually demand laborious preconcentration/microextraction steps to increase the concentration of Hg2+ or to eliminate matrix effects, to achieve LODs comparable to the one achieved in this work [6]. Atomic spectrometries are widely used instrumental techniques for the determination of Hg2+, with inherently low LODs and excellent sensitivities. Nevertheless, their use require extensive sample pre-treatments, since these detectors tend to be highly susceptible to the presence of interfering species. Thus, for example, sub-nmol L−1 LODs have been achieved with CV-AFS, but a previous microextraction step was needed [7]. The same is valid for electrothermal atomic absorption measurements, which are of extended use but require preconcentration steps to achieve LODs comparable with those obtained using 8-HQ-GQDs [9]. In this context, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry is currently on of the most widely applied techniques the for direct quantification of Hg2+ in aqueous samples, achieving LODs well below our value [63]. However, the high acquisition and maintenance cost of this technique represents a significant drawback regarding its application, which can be superseded by more cost-effective alternatives, such as the development hereby presented.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Front-face fluorescence measurements with real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practical application of the functionalized GQDs, the probe was tested for the determination of Hg2+ in tap, river, underground, and dam water samples under the optimized conditions described before. We encountered significant UV shielding effects for the probe when employing the typical right angle fluorescence technique. This represented an important inconvenience, because the linear relationship between relative intensities and analyte concentration (Equation (1)) requires an analyte-free medium for a calibration curve, making it difficult to apply a standard addition calibration method directly on the samples. Fig. S6 in the ESI shows the ability of front-face measurements to minimize reflected light, scattered radiation, depolarization effects, and shielding caused by the longer optical path lengths involved in classical right-angle measurements [64]. Accordingly, the front-face fluorescence technique became a fundamental tool to surpass these drawbacks, allowing for the elimination of shielding effects in real samples.

As a result of negligible shielding effects achieved by the use of front-face fluorescence measurements, an external calibration curve was used in all cases (Fig. 6). To further assess the accuracy of the method, recovery studies were carried out for each of the samples measured by front-face fluorescence, and the results were confirmed by CV-AFS, as shown in Table 2 . These data show that, despite the fact that the concentrations of Hg2+ detected by CV-AFS fall below the LOD obtained by the functionalized nanomaterial, the probe remains highly accurate, as demonstrated by the recovery assays, which yielded results that ranged from 96.4 to 107%. This proves the reliability and robustness of the current probe for the determination of Hg2+, even in matrices with considerable concentrations of organic and inorganic compounds, such as those found in natural waters. Furthermore, the validity of the CV-AFS measurements was confirmed by the good results obtained in the corresponding recovery assays.

A fast, sensitive, selective, and interfering species-free fluorescence probe was developed based on electrochemically-obtained GQDs functionalized with 8-HQ for the quantification of Hg2+ in laboratory and real samples. The use of the electrochemical exfoliation from a graphene foam led to a rapid, relatively simple, and clean synthesis of graphene nanostructures without the need of tedious dialysis or chromatography for size-selective discrimination. It was demonstrated that, although the as-obtained nanomaterial presented an excellent sensitivity toward the presence of low concentrations of Hg2+, it lacked selectivity. Accordingly, GQDs were functionalized with 8-HQ in order to significantly improve the selectivity upon Hg ions. Thus, with a judicious selection of the pH and the incorporation of EDTA to eliminate the interference of Fe3+, the modified GQDs probe demonstrated to be successfully applied for the determination of Hg2+ in multiple water sources including tap, river, underground, and dam waters with minimal sample treatment. The optimized method yielded a LOD of 2.4 nmol L−1 and was comparable or better than other GQD fluorescence-based Hg2+ probes, especially considering the simplicity and straightforwardness provided by the electrochemical synthesis. Furthermore, the accuracy of the method was confirmed by the recovery analysis of spiked samples and with the parallel experiments performed with CV-AFS used for further verification of these results. Finally, the synthesized nanomaterial and its optimized application as part of a complete analytical method can be considered a useful, fast, and cost-effective alternative for the environmental analysis of Hg2+.

Mauricio Llaver: carried put all the analytical measurements. Santiago D. Barrionuevo: Formal analysis, synthesized GQDs and performed and analyze XPS spectra. Eduardo Prieto: took AFM images. Rodolfo G. Wuilloud: Supervision, supervised and contributed with the selectivity of the probe.
